title: 1981-04-16_Pat_106.txt
key_claims: The Tagore vision successfully synthesizes Western and Eastern religions. From the West (Judaism/Christianity), it takes the idea of man's Covenant to be the custodian of the Garden (the Earth) and the concept of the Suffering Servant.
From the East (Pan-Indian thought), it takes the idea of the spiritual element suffering for *all* life and the concept of "withdrawal" from the world rather than active punishment.
If humanity destroys the ecosphere, we are breaking the fundamental Covenant with God. Tagore's injuries represent the karmic fallout of this severed covenant.

Dick views Tagore as the ultimate personification of spirituality—not just a philosophical or theological theory, but a real, literal, perfected human being who teaches compassion and the sparing of pain.
recurring_concepts: **Quasi-Pantheism**: Dick clarifies his view is *not* true Pantheism. Though the divine element lives in the ecosphere, it comes from a transcendent "home" to which it can withdraw if killed.
reading_excerpt: The Tagore vision successfully synthesizes Western and Eastern religions. From the West (Judaism/Christianity), it takes the idea of man's Covenant to be the custodian of the Garden (the Earth) and the concept of the Suffering Servant.
